article.title: US Transportation Department Launches Initiative to Put Small Modular Reactors on Commercial Ships
article.summary: MARAD issued a federal RFI on May 7 seeking industry input on developing commercially viable SMRs for civilian vessels, citing a strategic disadvantage against global competitors advancing maritime nuclear propulsion.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

The U.S. Maritime Administration (MARAD) issued a formal Request for Information on May 7, 2026, seeking industry input on developing small modular reactors for commercial ships. The notice, published in the Federal Register under docket MARAD-2026-0729, describes an effort to build "a U.S.-built scalable, repeatable, commercially viable, system-centric, small modular reactor (SMR)" for deployment in the marine transportation system. Public comments are due by August 5, 2026.

The initiative was announced by Transportation Secretary Sean P. Duffy alongside MARAD Administrator Stephen M. Carmel, and advances President Trump's April 2025 executive order on [Restoring America's Maritime Dominance](https://www.whitehouse.gov/maritimemight/).

## What We Know

The RFI targets a full system-transition, not a one-off demonstration. Carmel framed the government's role explicitly: "To successfully introduce SMRs, we must view this through a system-transition lens rather than just as a technology demonstration. We are seeking critical insights on how the government can help reduce systemic uncertainty, align regulatory structures, and enable the market conditions necessary for private capital and operators to scale these groundbreaking technologies."

Duffy said the goal is practical and security-driven: "By partnering with industry experts and outside-the-box thinkers to develop a strong SMR model, we will deliver a state-of-the-art energy source that cuts costs and bolsters national security."

The [American Nuclear Society](https://www.ans.org/news/2026-05-12/article-8024/transportation-department-seeks-to-develop-smrs-for-commercial-shipping/) reports that commercial ships typically require between 10 MW and 80 MW of propulsion power, a range that falls within many current SMR designs. Maritime nuclear propulsion is already in widespread use for military vessels but has seen almost no commercial adoption, in part because existing systems have been expensive and space-intensive.

MARAD listed four core topics for the RFI: identifying pathways toward repeatable deployment rather than one-off demonstrations; defining requirements for commercially viable maritime nuclear technology; assessing integration across shipbuilding, operations, and logistics networks; and supporting propulsion choices driven by lifecycle economics. The agency also flagged the need to develop frameworks for liability, insurance, port acceptance, workforce credentialing, and regulatory alignment with international standards including IMO's SOLAS conventions.

Coordinating agencies include the U.S. Coast Guard, the Nuclear Regulatory Commission, and the Department of Energy. The Coast Guard established a Maritime Nuclear Policy Division within its Office of Design and Engineering Standards in December 2025 to coordinate nuclear technology integration policies.

The strategic rationale cited in the Federal Register notice is direct: "Global competitors are advancing the integration of nuclear propulsion into the broader maritime industry, including shipyards, ports, insurance regimes, and logistics networks, which places the U.S. at a strategic disadvantage in the absence of domestic SMR development."

The initiative fits into a broader maritime policy push by the Trump administration. Executive Order 14269, signed April 9, 2025, requires a Maritime Action Plan to expand commercial shipbuilding capacity. The White House notes that the [US currently builds less than 1% of the world's commercial ships](https://www.whitehouse.gov/maritimemight/) and has only 8 active shipbuilding yards.

Global competition is already moving on maritime nuclear. In March 2026, South Korean shipbuilder HD Hyundai and the American Bureau of Shipping signed a joint development agreement to advance a conceptual design for a [16,000-TEU container ship powered by a 100-MW SMR](https://interestingengineering.com/energy/hd-hyundai-abs-plan-nuclear-powered-cargo-ship). ABS granted the propulsion concept an Approval in Principle at Gastech 2026. Matthew Muller of ABS said the collaboration would "comprehensively verify the safety, efficiency, and eco-friendliness of next-generation propulsion solutions."

The American Nuclear Society's Operations and Power Division established a Civilian Nuclear Maritime Task Force in 2025, focused on international codes and standards, regulatory harmonization, workforce development, and public policy engagement.

## What We Don't Know

The RFI is an information-gathering exercise, not a commitment to fund or build anything. MARAD has not identified a preferred reactor design, a shipyard partner, or a funding mechanism. It is unclear how the agency would navigate the significant port-access and international-treaty obstacles that contributed to the retirement of the NS Savannah — the only U.S. civilian nuclear vessel, which operated from 1962 to 1971 under President Eisenhower's Atoms for Peace program before being decommissioned as uneconomical.

The timeline for translating RFI responses into an actual procurement or demonstration program has not been disclosed. The federal notice states that any future actions would be pursued "in alignment with applicable international standards, protocols, and development processes, including engagement through the International Maritime Organization," but no IMO process is currently underway that would approve nuclear-powered commercial vessels for unrestricted global port access.

It also remains unclear which SMR vendors, if any, have technology ready for the marine environment, where reactors face corrosion, vibration, and space constraints not present in land-based plants.
